figures.caption: Figure 1 | Overview of our Fast-dVLM. (a) Fast-dVLM achieves comparable accuracy to AR VLM baselines on MMMU-Pro-V with a substantial speedup. (b) Benchmark comparison against the Qwen2.5-VL-3B backbone, showing near-lossless accuracy across diverse tasks. (c) Our combined approach achieves up to 6.18× end-to-end speedup compared to the AR baseline. All throughput measurements are conducted on a single NVIDIA H100 GPU.
Table 4 | Inference acceleration on MMMU-Pro-V. Each row progressively adds one optimization on top of the previous. SpeedUp is relative to the AR baseline.

Figure 7 | Qualitative comparison on an MMMU-Pro-V math reasoning problem. Both the AR baseline (Qwen2.5-VL-3B) and Fast-dVLM (speculative decoding) arrive at the correct answer, while Fast-dVLM achieves 1.6× faster decoding speed (77.7 vs. 47.4 tokens/s).

Figure 2 | Two AR-to-diffusion conversion strategies. (a) The two-stage strategy first converts the LLM backbone via text-only diffusion fine-tuning (Stage 1), then fine-tunes all components on multimodal data (Stage 2). (b) The direct path converts the full VLM in a single multimodal stage.

Figure 3 | Training architecture and attention mask for [w𝑡; x] with block size ?̂? = 2. The noisy stream w𝑡 contains only text tokens; vision tokens appear exclusively in the clean stream x (vision-efficient concatenation). ℳN2N: block-diagonal attention for parallel denoising. ℳN2C: noisy tokens attend to preceding clean context including vision tokens. ℳC2C: token-level causal attention on the clean stream, enabling joint AR loss training and AR decoding at inference.
Figure 8 | Additional qualitative examples spanning art style recognition, celebrity identification, and chart QA. Fast-dVLM produces accurate responses while maintaining high throughput.

Figure 6 | Speculative decoding throughput across block sizes, comparing linear and quadratic variants. (a) Tokens per NFE increases with block size for both variants. (b) Wall-clock TPS peaks at block size 16 for both variants; the quadratic variant’s 𝑂(𝐵2) cost becomes dominant at larger block sizes.

Figure 9 | Qualitative examples of Fast-dVLM (speculative decoding) on embodied and physical AI tasks: autonomous driving scene understanding and robotic manipulation instruction. For each example, we report the generated response length (Gen. Len), decoding throughput (Tok./sec), and tokens per decoding step (Tok./step). Fast-dVLM produces detailed, step-by-step reasoning for both tasks while maintaining high throughput (∼73 tokens/s) and a Tok./step ratio above 1.68.
